summary: Software engineers are drawn to systematic, structural approaches to management, but rigid organizational structures often fail when applied to real teams. While rules of thumb like optimal team sizes (7-8 direct reports) have value, experienced managers know that living organizations constantly shift due to departures, hiring bursts, and individual needs. Applying engineering concepts like APIs to team interactions ignores how humans actually work together—trading favors, exploiting informal channels, and adapting to circumstances. Leaders who focus too heavily on optimizing systems risk losing good people who don't fit perfectly into their structures. The key is balancing organizational principles with flexibility, accepting imperfect structures to retain people over the long term.
key_points: Engineering managers don't scale well beyond 7-8 direct reports, but organizations with only 2-3 reports create limbo states between IC and manager roles
Clean organizational tree structures quickly fall apart due to departures, sticky manager-team relationships, and uneven hiring patterns
Applying engineering analogies like APIs to team interactions fails because humans will always find and exploit informal channels (Hyrum's Law applies to organizations)
Optimizing for system efficiency can inadvertently create friction for people who don't fit the structure, causing attrition
Startup experience can mislead managers into thinking they can perfectly tune structures, but stable organizations require accepting imperfection to retain people over years
money_quotes: Software engineers are attracted to formulas, algorithms, and structures.
The teams need to have some coherence of vision and purpose; hiring is never a steady drip but comes and goes in bursts.

APIs are a fine concept but the idea that team interactions can be strictly defined by an API is as laughable as the idea that programmers won't figure out a way to exploit every undocumented feature you provide in one.
Hyrum's Law applies as much to engineering organizations as it does to software.
When you see the world in systems, interactions, and efficiencies, you can trick yourself into believing that the humans inside of these systems will be happier if the system is well-organized.
The people in the system are much trickier than you are imagining, and the structure that accounts for their skills and happiness will have organic features as much as centrally-planned ones.

When you are thinking in terms of years and not months, it becomes important to accept that you may hang out in imperfect structures for many months or even longer, because you are trying to hang onto people for years.
Listen to your people, and don't be afraid to break your perfect structures to accommodate their needs.
key_insight: Organizational structures should be treated as living, imperfect systems that bend to accommodate people's needs rather than rigid frameworks that force people to conform.
